items.q: What does gravitational field strength at a point represent?
items.correct: The force per unit mass that would act on a test mass placed at that point.
items.misconception: The total gravitational force at that point regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: The mass of the object creating the field.
items.why: Students forget the 'per unit mass' (or per unit charge) part of field strength definitions, conflating the field with the resulting force.

items.q: What does specific latent heat measure?
items.correct: The energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature.
items.misconception: The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 degree.
items.random_wrong: The temperature at which a substance melts or boils.
items.why: Students confuse specific latent heat with specific heat capacity because both involve 'heat' and 'kg', missing the no-temperature-change aspect of latent heat.

items.q: What is the difference between brittle and ductile materials?
items.correct: Brittle materials fracture with little or no plastic deformation; ductile materials undergo significant plastic deformation before breaking.
items.misconception: Brittle materials are weak and ductile materials are strong.
items.random_wrong: Brittle materials are metals and ductile materials are non-metals.
items.why: Students conflate brittleness with weakness, missing that a brittle material (like glass) can have very high tensile strength but no warning before failure.

items.q: Which is the closest planet to the Sun?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mercury. “Venus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's A star. “A planet” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many planets orbit our Sun?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
